skill.name: go-concurrency-patterns
skill.description: Guides Go developers through practical goroutine, channel, context, worker pool, pipeline, synchronization, and race detection patterns.
skill.summary: Apply production Go concurrency patterns with clear examples and review guidance.
skill.author: wshobson

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported high risk, but review found Markdown code fences, Go import blocks, local variable names, and documentation links. No prompt injection, malicious instructions, data exfiltration, or executable installer behavior was found. The remaining risk is low and limited to example Go commands and network-related sample code.

low_findings.title: Markdown and Go Syntax Flagged as Executable Scripts
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: Static script and backtick detections occur inside Markdown tables or fenced Go examples. These lines document Go syntax and are not skill-executed code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged patterns are visible Markdown delimiters, inline code markers, or Go import blocks. No generated script file or runtime execution path is present.

low_findings.title: High-Risk Keyword Matches Are Benign Go Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The high-risk keyword detections are local channel variables, function declarations, and the frontmatter description. No command-and-control, reconnaissance, or weak cryptography behavior is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The C2 hits are variables named c2 and c3 in a fan-in example. The other lines are descriptive text or worker function signatures with no security-sensitive operation.

low_findings.title: Path Traversal Detection Matches Ellipsis Text
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The filesystem detections point to log messages ending with ellipses. They do not reference file paths, path joins, or user-controlled filesystem access.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Both locations are fmt.Printf status messages in a graceful shutdown example. There is no path traversal sequence used as a filesystem path.

low_findings.title: Official Documentation Links Are Low Risk
low_findings.description: FALSE_POSITIVE: The hardcoded URLs are references to official Go documentation and Go by Example. They are learning resources, not network calls made by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are static Markdown links to reputable Go learning resources. No evidence shows credential transmission, tracking endpoints, or automatic fetch behavior.

low_findings.title: Race Detector Commands Require Normal User Review
low_findings.description: NEEDS_REVIEW: The skill documents go test, go build, and go run race detector commands. They are legitimate Go commands but should be run only in trusted projects.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are standard Go tooling and contain no shell interpolation or remote endpoint. They still execute project code when a user chooses to run them.

content.value_statement: Concurrent Go code can leak goroutines, race on shared state, or ignore cancellation. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through proven goroutine, channel, context, worker pool, and shutdown patterns.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use goroutines, channels, mutexes, wait groups, and context cancellation.
Provides worker pool, fan-out fan-in, semaphore, graceful shutdown, and errgroup patterns.
Shows how to coordinate goroutine lifecycles and close result channels safely.
Covers sync.Map and sharded map approaches for concurrent data access.
Highlights Go race detector commands for testing concurrent behavior.
Lists practical do and do not guidance for avoiding common concurrency bugs.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not an automated race detector.
It does not benchmark code or prove a concurrency design is optimal.
It cannot guarantee third-party task functions are safe to run concurrently.
It focuses on Go concurrency, not distributed systems architecture.

use_cases.title: Design a Worker Pool
use_cases.description: Plan bounded job processing with cancellation, result collection, and controlled goroutine counts.

use_cases.title: Review Concurrency Bugs
use_cases.description: Check goroutine lifecycles, channel closing, shared state access, and cancellation paths before release.

use_cases.title: Teach Go Patterns
use_cases.description: Use concise examples to explain channels, pipelines, semaphores, and graceful shutdown to a team.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Basic Pattern
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner planning a small concurrent task
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a Go concurrency pattern for this task. Explain whether I should use goroutines, channels, a mutex, or a worker pool.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Worker Pool
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Go worker pool for this workload. Include cancellation, bounded concurrency, result collection, and safe channel closing.

prompt_templates.title: Review for Races
prompt_templates.prompt: Review my Go concurrent design for race conditions, goroutine leaks, blocked sends, unsafe channel closes, and missing context cancellation.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Production Shutdown
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a production-grade graceful shutdown plan for my Go service. Cover signal handling, deadlines, worker cleanup, and error propagation.

output_examples.input: I need to process thousands of jobs without starting unlimited goroutines.
output_examples.output: A bounded worker pool recommendation with cancellation handling, result aggregation, and clear guidance on buffer sizing.

output_examples.input: My pipeline sometimes hangs after one stage returns early.
output_examples.output: A review explaining likely blocked sends, missing context checks, and how to close or drain channels safely.

output_examples.input: I want to shut down a Go service without losing in-flight work.
output_examples.output: A shutdown sequence covering signal capture, context deadlines, worker stop paths, wait groups, and timeout behavior.

best_practices: Start every goroutine with a clear cancellation or completion path.
Use context cancellation for request-scoped work and coordinated shutdown.
Run the Go race detector on tests that exercise concurrent paths.
anti_patterns: Starting unbounded goroutines for user-controlled workloads.
Closing channels from receivers or from multiple senders.
Using time.Sleep as a synchronization mechanism.

faq.question: Does this skill execute Go code?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. Users decide whether to run any commands or code.

faq.question: Can it detect all race conditions?
faq.answer: No. It can guide reviews and suggest race detector use, but runtime testing is still required.

faq.question: Does it cover context cancellation?
faq.answer: Yes. Context cancellation appears across worker, pipeline, semaphore, errgroup, and shutdown examples.

faq.question: Is it useful for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes. It starts with core primitives and then moves into production patterns.

faq.question: Does it recommend sync.Map for every map?
faq.answer: No. It frames sync.Map as useful for frequent reads and infrequent writes.

faq.question: Can Claude Code or Codex use it during code review?
faq.answer: Yes. It gives focused review criteria for goroutine leaks, races, channel use, and shutdown behavior.
